title: Lomatium alatum
intro: Lomatium alatum, also known as Lewis' desert parsley, is a perennial herbaceous plant native to the western United States and Canada. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities that have been traditionally used to treat colds, flu, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Lomatium alatum include sesquiterpene lactones, alkaloids, and glycosides, which are thought to contribute to its health benefits. Lomatium alatum is often used in herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, and infusions, and is also used in combination with other herbs to create respiratory and immune system support formulas.
taxonomy: Lomatium alatum is classified as a perennial plant, specifically belonging to the family Apiaceae. It is also known as the winged biscuitroot or alpine lomatium. Within the Apiaceae family, Lomatium alatum is part of the genus Lomatium. The genus Lomatium is a group of flowering plants native to North America. The species Lomatium alatum is often found growing in mountainous regions.
uses: Lomatium alatum has been traditionally used in folk medicine, particularly by Native American tribes. The plant's roots and leaves are believed to possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate various health issues. Its medicinal uses include treating respiratory problems, such as coughs and colds, as well as relieving digestive issues and skin conditions. The plant's active compounds may also have antiviral and antifungal properties, contributing to its potential therapeutic applications. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal potential and its effectiveness in treating specific health conditions.

side_effects: Lomatium alatum, like other members of the carrot family, can cause gastrointestinal issues, such as nausea and diarrhea, when ingested in large quantities.
